content.problemStatement:
Today, you finally have a chance to compete with a famous coder like Chef and prove your mettle.

Chef has nnn distinct points in 2-D plane, i.e. there are never two points with both xxx and yyy coordinate equal and there are no two points with the same xxx coordinate. Consider a subset of the above nnn points which can be arranged as a sequence p1,…,pkp_1, \dots, p_kp1​,…,pk​, such that xp1<xp2<⋯<xpkx_{p_1} < x_{p_2} < \dots < x_{p_k}xp1​​<xp2​​<⋯<xpk​​ and each point pip_ipi​, where iii is in the range between 3 and kkk (both inclusive), lies below or on the line passing between points pi−1p_{i-1}pi−1​ and pi−2p_{i-2}pi−2​. Chef likes these kinds of subsets. So he wants to find the size of the largest of such subsets.


Input Format
First line of the input contains one integer TTT - number of tests. TTT tests follow.


First line of each test contains one integer NNN - number of points.


The next NNN lines of each test case contain integer point coordinates xix_ixi​, yiy_iyi​.



Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer in a separate line corresponding to the largest subset size.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases is at most 2000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the number of points. The next N lines contain two integers each, representing the x and y coordinates of a point.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer, the size of the largest subset of points that can be arranged in a sequence such that each point lies below or on the line passing between the two previous points.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: We can get the sequence (1, 1) - (4, 2) - (9, 3), where point (9, 3) lies below the line between points (1, 1) and (4, 2).

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to find the longest increasing subsequence of points. We first sort the points by their x-coordinates. Then, for each point, we find the maximum length of the subsequence that ends at that point by considering all previous points. We use the fact that a point lies below or on the line passing between two previous points if the slope of the line between the current point and the previous point is less than or equal to the slope of the line between the previous two points.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) — we loop over each test case, and for each test case, we loop over each point and consider all previous points
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — we need to store the points and the dynamic programming table
